provenance.source_corpus: An experimental study of how variation in deer density affects vegetation and songbird assemblages of recently harvested boreal forests

source_spans.text: reducing deer densities ≤7.5 deer·km –2 initiated the restoration of balsam fir ( Abies balsamea (L.) Mill.) forests and increased the regeneration of paper birch ( Betula papyrifera Marshall).
reducing deer densities ≤7.5 deer·km –2 initiated the restoration of balsam fir ( Abies balsamea (L.) Mill.) forests and increased the regeneration of paper birch ( Betula papyrifera Marshall).
reducing deer densities ≤7.5 deer·km –2 initiated the restoration of balsam fir ( Abies balsamea (L.) Mill.) forests and increased the regeneration of paper birch ( Betula papyrifera Marshall).
Increasing birch ground cover from 10% to 20% increased songbird total abundance, species richness, and diversity by 17%, 39%, and 31%, respectively.
Increasing birch ground cover from 10% to 20% increased songbird total abundance, species richness, and diversity by 17%, 39%, and 31%, respectively.
Increasing birch ground cover from 10% to 20% increased songbird total abundance, species richness, and diversity by 17%, 39%, and 31%, respectively.
Increasing birch ground cover from 10% to 20% increased songbird total abundance, species richness, and diversity by 17%, 39%, and 31%, respectively.
Six years of deer control after tree harvest had measurable and significant indirect, vegetation-mediated, positive effects on songbird richness and diversity.
Six years of deer control after tree harvest had measurable and significant indirect, vegetation-mediated, positive effects on songbird richness and diversity.
browse-sensitive plant species in the field and shrub layers were more abundant and the complexity of the vertical structure was greater under reduced deer density.
